title: vector
description: The default DSA container: push_back, size, insert/erase, and the crucial safety difference between [] and at().
objectives: Declare, grow, and shrink a vector
Use size(), empty(), insert(), and erase()
Know the safety difference between [] and at()
Understand, at a high level, how vector grows internally

sections.text: Standard Template Library — STL already gave us a glimpse: vector<int> numbers; with push_back(). Let's go deeper.

sections.code:
vector<int> a;                  // empty vector
vector<int> b = {1, 2, 3};       // initialized with values
vector<int> c(5, 0);             // 5 elements, all initialized to 0

sections.text: 5. [] vs at() — A Crucial Safety Difference

Recall Arrays: numbers[5] here would be undefined behavior, exactly like an out-of-bounds raw array access — no error, just unreliable behavior. at(5) is different: it performs a bounds check and, connecting to Exception Handling, throws a std::out_of_range exception that can actually be caught. The exact wording of e.what() can vary by compiler/standard library, but the behavior — a catchable, safe error — is guaranteed.

rows: No bounds checking — out-of-bounds access is undefined behavior
Bounds-checked — throws std::out_of_range on invalid access
Slightly slower (the check has a cost)

sections.text: Both take an iterator position, connecting directly to STL Iterators — begin() + 2 means "the position two steps after the start."

Internally, vector keeps a block of memory (recall new[] from Dynamic Memory Allocation) somewhat larger than what's currently needed. push_back() usually just fills the next open slot; occasionally, when that block fills up completely, vector allocates a bigger block behind the scenes and copies everything over. This happens automatically — you never manage it yourself — and is why vector is sometimes described as having amortized constant-time push_back(), even though any single call could occasionally trigger a full resize.

sections.code: vector<vector<int>> grid(3, vector<int>(4, 0)); // 3 rows, 4 columns, all 0

sections.text: This is only a preview — a vector of vectors gives a resizable version of the 2D Arrays grid from earlier, still indexed as grid[row][col]. We'll return to this pattern once we're deeper into using STL containers for actual DSA problems.

sections.title: An expanding accordion folder
sections.text: An ordinary array is a fixed box — once full, that's it. vector is more like an accordion folder that quietly expands to a bigger size when you keep adding papers, without you needing to go buy a new folder and manually move everything over yourself.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using [] when there's real uncertainty about whether an index is valid — at() is the safer choice when bounds aren't already guaranteed.
Calling pop_back() or accessing an element on an empty vector — check empty() or size() first.
Assuming push_back() always takes exactly the same amount of time — it's amortized constant time, but an occasional call does a full resize underneath.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between [] and at() on a vector?
questions.answer: [] performs no bounds checking, so an out-of-range access is undefined behavior; at() checks bounds and throws a std::out_of_range exception on an invalid index.

questions.question: What does push_back() do?
questions.answer: Adds a new element to the end of the vector, resizing its internal storage automatically if needed.

questions.question: What does it mean that vector has 'amortized constant-time' push_back()?
questions.answer: Most push_back() calls are fast, simply filling the next open slot, but occasionally the internal storage is full and must be resized and copied — averaged over many calls, the cost per call is still effectively constant.

questions.question: How would you create a 2D grid using vector?
questions.answer: A vector of vectors, e.g. vector<vector<int>> grid(rows, vector<int>(cols, 0));, indexed as grid[row][col].

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: vector is a resizable array — the default STL container for most DSA problems. push_back() and pop_back() grow and shrink it from the end, size() and empty() describe its current state, and insert()/erase() work at any iterator position. The key safety detail is [] versus at(): [] performs no bounds checking, matching the undefined behavior of a raw array from Arrays, while at() checks bounds and throws a catchable std::out_of_range exception instead. Internally, vector over-allocates and resizes automatically as needed, giving push_back() amortized constant-time performance.

sections.title: Trick to Remember vector
sections.text: vector = an accordion folder, expands automatically. [] = fast, no safety net. at() = slower, but catches you if you fall (throws, catchable).
